1: yhwh lōʾ-gābah libbî wĕlōʾ-rāmû ʿênay wĕlōʾ-hālaktî bigdōlôt ûbĕniplāʾôt mimmennî — "LORD, my heart is not proud; my eyes are not lifted up in arrogance; I do not walk in matters too great or too marvelous for me" — the three negations form a triple self-renunciation. gābah lēb (proud heart, lit. "the heart is high") — the opposite of the humble heart that fears the LORD (Prov 16:5). rāmû ʿênay (my eyes are lifted, i.e., haughty eyes) — the high look of arrogance (Ps 18:27). niplāʾôt (wonders, things too wonderful/marvelous for me) — the same word used for YHWH's wondrous works (Ps 111:4; Job 42:3); the psalmist deliberately steps back from presuming to comprehend what belongs to divine mystery.
2: ʾim-lōʾ šiwwîtî wĕdômamtî napšî kĕgāmûl ʿălê ʾimmô kĕgāmûl ʿālay napšî — "surely I have settled and silenced my soul; like a weaned child resting with its mother — like a weaned child, my soul is at rest within me" — šiwwîtî (I have stilled, made even, leveled — Piel of šāwāh) + dômamtî (I have silenced, made quiet — Hiphil of dāmam). kĕgāmûl ʿălê ʾimmô (like a weaned child with/upon his mother) — the key word is gāmûl (weaned child, from gāmal, to wean, also "to deal fully, to recompense"). A weaned child is no longer crying for milk — it has moved beyond the urgency of infancy into a resting relationship. The child rests not because it has received what it wanted but because the relationship itself is enough. This is the psalm's whole theology: the soul quieted not by receiving everything it desired but by settling into YHWH's presence without demand.
3: yaḥēl yiśrāʾēl ʾel-yhwh mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām — "O Israel, put your hope in the LORD, now and forever" — the closing call to Israel echoes Ps 130:7 verbatim (the same yaḥēl yiśrāʾēl ʾel-yhwh), creating a doublet between the two psalms. The individual soul's quieting (v2) becomes the model for Israel's collective posture: the nation is called to the same weaned-child rest in the LORD. mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām (from now and unto eternity) — the standard eschatological formula (Ps 113:2; 115:18; 121:8) extends this quiet trust into permanent reality.
